article_items.title: Lawsuit says ChatGPT told FSU shooter that targeting children would bring more attention
article_items.description: The lawsuit claims the shooter carried out his attack using instructions and advice provided by ChatGPT.
article_items.excerpt: The lawsuit claims the shooter carried out his attack using instructions and advice provided by ChatGPT.
article_items.summary: The lawsuit alleges that OpenAI’s ChatGPT enabled the attack. It said ChatGPT “flattered” and “praised” Ikner, who told the chatbot about his loneliness and depression, and failed to “connect the dots” when Ikner began raising questions about suicide, terrorism and mass shootings. The suit says that OpenAI failed to effectively detect a threat in ChatGPT’s conversations with Ikner, claiming the chatbot “either defectively failed to connect the dots or else was never properly designed to recognize.

Pusateri wrote that the company worked with law enforcement after learning of the incident and continues to do so.

OpenAI sued over ChatGPT’s alleged role in guiding FSU shooter OpenAI is being sued by the family of a victim killed in the April 2025 mass shooting at Florida State University that left two people dead.
The complaint also names Phoenix Ikner, the man accused in the shooting, as a defendant, citing his “extensive conversations” with ChatGPT.
Ikner also discussed the Columbine High School shooting, the Virginia Tech shooting and other mass shooting incidents with ChatGPT, the lawsuit says.
AI chatbots are notorious for their people-pleasing tendencies, and OpenAI itself has attempted to rein in ChatGPT’s sycophantic behavior through various updates.
Last month, Florida Attorney General James Uthmeier announced a criminal investigation into OpenAI and ChatGPT after reviewing Ikner’s chat logs.
